abstract: Electrochemical oxidation of 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) at the anode offers a promising, energy-efficient route for simultaneous hydrogen production and the synthesis of value-added chemicals. While this process operates at significantly lower potentials (~0.1 V vs. RHE) compared to conventional water splitting, the underlying reaction mechanisms and the identity of key intermediates have remained elusive. In this study, we utilize in situ Raman and attenuated total reflectance infrared (ATR-IR) spectroscopy, combined with isotope labeling and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, to investigate HMF oxidation on Au-modified Ni foam. We identify two critical, pH-dependent gem-diol intermediates: the 5-(dihydroxymethyl)furan-2-methanol anion (DHMFM$^-$) and dianion (DHMFM$^{2-}$). Our findings reveal that the dianion intermediate facilitates H$_2$ generation via a hydride (H$^-$) transfer mechanism at low potentials. Conversely, the monoanion intermediate undergoes a proton (H$^+$) transfer to yield H$_2$O. We demonstrate that increasing the electrode potential accelerates the oxidation rate of DHMFM$^-$, which shifts the chemical equilibrium away from DHMFM$^{2-}$, thereby supressing and eventually terminating anodic hydrogen production. By resolving the competition between these two pathways and capturing the role of the gem-diol dianion, this work provides a fundamental framework for the rational design of electrocatalytic systems for biomass-derived aldehyde upgrading and hydrogen evolution.
